detailed_analysis: The six core sync-engine files are in fundamentally good structural shape — small modules, narrow public surfaces, dataclasses with explicit (de)serialization, no hardcoded secrets, no injection vectors, no global mutable state, and a clean acyclic import graph. The two largest files (discovery.py at 507 lines, sync.py at 265 lines) are right at or above the project's 500-line module convention, but neither is a true God Module: they each have one clear responsibility. The main concerns are not stylistic — they are semantic. (1) canonical.py does NOT canonicalize: is_private is the only invariant enforced, and the JSON written to disk is json.dumps(..., sort_keys=True) which produces stable key order but does nothing about whitespace inside body strings, list ordering of tools/disallowed_tools, None-vs-missing semantics, or normalization of name. Two semantically-equal canonicals coming from different tools can therefore round-trip to different SHA-256 digests — a real invariant gap. (2) load_state accepts schema_version=3 but the surrounding log/docstring still say schema_version=2. The rebuild-on-mismatch branch silently discards user state on any version drift. (3) sync.py's sync_once and _reconcile_new_groups swallow per-pair Exceptions with logging.exception(...) and continue. The changed return value conflates 'no work' with 'work attempted and silently failed'. (4) Several functions in discovery.py carry 6–8 parameters because the discovery dict, blocked set, and state dict are threaded through every internal call. (5) Path is used as the primitive everywhere; there is no ArtifactPath / SharedFilePath distinction, and slot: str | None is plumbed through AgenticToolInfo, PlannedTarget, RenderResult, AgenticToolState. (6) The round-trip parse(render(canonical)) is not enforced anywhere. The codebase is structured to allow asymmetry to slip in unnoticed.

findings.problem: The module is named 'canonical' but the only normalization performed before persisting is sort_keys=True. No invariants are enforced on: list ordering of tools/disallowed_tools; None-vs-omitted for model/effort/permission_mode; trailing-newline / CRLF normalization on body; whitespace stripping on name/description. There is no canonicalize(dict) function and no canonical_equal(a, b) helper. Two semantically-equal artifacts coming from two different tools can produce two distinct canonical JSONs and two distinct SHA-256 digests.

Returns None for three semantically-distinct cases: canonical never existed, canonical exists but is corrupt JSON, canonical is valid JSON but not an object. Case (b) is a real partial-write recovery scenario but is collapsed into 'no canonical yet' by callers, which then re-mint a fresh canonical and overwrite the corrupted one.

Five distinct failure modes (file missing, JSON parse error, not-a-dict, wrong schema_version, missing customization_artifacts) all collapse to 'return {}'. An empty state dict drives the rest of sync_once to treat every artifact on disk as a newly-discovered orphan and adopt it — fan out unintended writes across all tools if a transient JSON corruption happens. No quarantine, no .bak, no metric.

findings.variant: Stale comment / docstring out of sync with code
findings.problem: The docstring claims 'schema_version=2 only' while the constant is STATE_SCHEMA_VERSION = 3.

Between tmp.write_text(...) and tmp.replace(path), the bytes are not fsynced; after the rename, the parent directory is not fsynced. A power loss can leave the renamed inode pointing at zero bytes on ext4 with data=writeback and on most Windows filesystems. Also, the tmp suffix '.{name}.tmp' is fixed — two interleaved writers to the same path will clobber each other's tmp file. This is the source of the corrupt-state cases CQ-02 / CQ-03 then mishandle.
507 lines, 7 over the project's 500-line convention. DiscoveryWalker has 13 methods clustered as: enumeration / per-cell walk, target / collision planning, state-owner blocking helpers.
65 lines, four early returns, two isinstance(file_layout, SharedKeyedMapLayout) branches (one for source, one per target), three distinct policies, and one I/O fan-out. Five responsibilities.

60 lines, nested for-inside-for with three independent block conditions (managed owner collision, unmanaged file collision, multi-pair planned-target collision). Mixes 'detect collisions' with 'mutate discovery'.
Three helpers carry the same trailing clump (pairs, blocked_pair_ids, state). Adding a new cross-cutting per-poll concern forces a signature change in 4–5 methods.

Five separate isinstance(..., SharedKeyedMapLayout) branches across two files. Each handles 'read this artifact's text' or 'compute this artifact's target' differently. Adding a third file layout would require finding and editing every site.

Discovery parses the source bytes into a canonical and then derives the projection target only from canonical['name'] via slugify. There is no check that parse(render(canonical_for_target_tool)) would round-trip back to the same canonical. The absence of any canonical_equal or sha256-round-trip check means symmetry is enforced only by external tests.
Bare except Exception followed by return. A transient parse failure of the shared MCP file therefore CAN cause every keyed-map slot on that tool to look removed and trigger downstream propagation.

findings.variant: Two catch-Exception + logging.exception sites in the orchestrator
findings.problem: Catches every per-pair Exception. There is no errors counter, no list of failed pair_ids, no flag in the return value. A daemon supervisor cannot tell 'all 50 pairs synced cleanly' from '49 cleanly, 1 corrupt for 100 polls in a row'.

88-line function with two phases (group, then per-group decide-and-merge) and a closure defined inside a for-loop that captures the loop variable indirectly via source_tool_by_pair. The closure-in-loop pattern is bug-prone (late binding).

agentic_tool_spec is already imported at the top of sync.py, so the inline 'from agents_sync.agentic_tool_spec import SharedKeyedMapLayout' is not a cycle-break — it is a forgotten artifact of an earlier refactor.

findings.variant: Cross-instance state mutated implicitly between calls
findings.problem: _blocked_pair_ids is reset and rebuilt on every sync_once call but lives as an instance attribute. If two threads ever call sync_once on the same Syncer they will read stale or partially-rebuilt data.

Four dataclasses and at least six function signatures carry the (path: Path, slot: str | None) pair. The pair is semantically one thing: a location at which an artifact lives. Also: AgenticToolState.path is str while every other site uses Path.

findings.variant: Wall-clock timestamp with no clock-skew handling
findings.problem: mtime_wins resolution uses a wall-clock timestamp with no monotonic fallback and no clock-skew tolerance. If the host's clock moves backwards (NTP correction, VM resume, dual-boot with Windows local-time bug), a freshly-written last_modified can compare older than an import snapshot — the import wins and live edits are silently overwritten.

findings.variant: TOCTOU around path.stat() after potential error
findings.problem: path.stat() is called unguarded after sha256_file(path). If the file is removed between the digest and the stat (artifact tree is user-writable and daemon runs concurrently), path.stat() raises FileNotFoundError, bubbling up to the per-pair Exception swallower.

verdict_summary: Engine is secure, acyclic, and well-organized at the module level; weakened by an unenforced canonicalization contract, silent state-rebuild on corruption, non-atomic 'atomic' writes, three long functions in discovery/sync, repeated isinstance-based layout dispatch (shotgun surgery), and pervasive (path, slot) primitive obsession.
